name: M.S. in Computer Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The degree has a clean 12-credit core (CS 610, 630, 631, 656, CS/DS 675) covering algorithms, OS, databases, networks and ML, plus an 18-credit elective pool of ~70 courses; deepestPrereqChain=3 evidences real sequencing (CS 656->646->647; CS 610->665/667/668), giving strong breadth-then-depth progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the ~70 electives into named concentration tracks (AI/ML, Security, Systems, Data, Software Engineering) so students see coherent pathways rather than a flat list.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: metrics report deepestPrereqChain=3, missingCourseCount=0 and no dangling references across 75 referenced courses, with well-formed ladders such as CS 645/646->647 and CS 610->668; the graph is healthy and neither over- nor under-constrained.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace vague gates like 'completion of bridge requirements' (CS 633) with explicit course lists so applicants can self-assess readiness.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the provided data despite the otherwise well-structured page.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish program learning outcomes tied to the core competency areas (algorithms, systems, ML, security).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits=30 against statedTotalCredits=30 with creditReconcileDelta=0, split transparently into a 12-credit core and 18-credit electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Consider carving an explicit required capstone or thesis (CS 700B/701B) out of the elective pool so every graduate completes an integrative experience.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page has parsed requirement groups (Core 12 cr / Elective 18 cr) with legible course titles and present descriptions, but lacks stated learning outcomes and any track labeling on the large elective list.
dimensions.suggestion: Add learning outcomes and concentration-track headings to the elective section for scannability.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The elective pool is highly current: CS 643 Cloud Computing, CS/DS 677 Deep Learning, DS 680 NLP, CS/DS 644 Big Data, CS 750 High Performance Computing, a full security suite (CS 645/646/647/708/755) and CS 756 mobile/sensor networks reflect contemporary practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Add explicit MLOps/data-engineering and generative-AI/LLM electives to cover the production-ML and foundation-model gaps.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are specific and informative (e.g., CS 643 naming AWS/Google platforms, CS 647 'learning by doing' offensive/defensive labs, CS 703 continuous optimization), with no thin entries observed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: With missingCourseCount=0 and explicit chains (CS 611 requires CS 610/435 or doctoral status; CS 668 requires CS 610 and CS 650), prerequisites are largely unambiguous; only phrases like 'completion of bridge requirements' remain vague.

missingTopics.rationale: CS/DS 675/677 teach modeling but no course covers deployment, serving, monitoring or lifecycle of ML systems.
CS/DS 644 Big Data exists, but modern batch/stream pipeline tooling (Spark/Kafka) and analytics engineering are not represented.
Theory is strong (CS 611 complexity, CS 665/667 algorithms) yet software correctness/verification is absent from the SE electives.
An increasingly standard graduate CS topic that does not appear among the 75 referenced courses.
The security suite is deep but differential privacy and federated learning are not covered alongside CS 708.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI / LLMs and agentic systems
emergingTrends.rationale: DS 680 NLP and CS/DS 677 Deep Learning provide a base to add a dedicated foundation-models/LLM course.
Complements the strong ML electives with the engineering and governance skills employers now expect.
CS 643 Cloud Computing could extend to containers/Kubernetes and serverless architectures.
Bridges the security track (CS 708) and ML track with federated learning and differential privacy.
Connects the languages (CS 635) and SE electives to LLM-assisted software development.

topSuggestions: Publish program-level learning outcomes on the catalog page.
Organize the ~70 electives into named concentration tracks (AI/ML, Security, Systems, Data, SE).
Add a generative-AI/LLM course and an MLOps/data-engineering elective to close production-ML gaps.
Make a capstone project or thesis (CS 700B/701B) explicitly required rather than optional within electives.
Standardize 'bridge requirements' into explicit prerequisite course lists.
